title: Trauma Care Access in Cook County
text:
← Back to projects
  
  
    Policy Memo
    Decision Matrix · BOTEC
    Public Health
  
  A Policy Memo: Reducing Geographic Disparities in Trauma Care Access in Cook County
  A policy memo diagnosing geographic disparities in trauma care access in Cook County through a five-step sequential model and evaluating four policy alternatives using a weighted decision matrix.



  
     Read Full Memo
  



  Year2025
  TypeCourse Assignment
  CoursePublic Policy Development & Process
  MethodsDecision Matrix · BOTEC



  Executive Summary
  There are severe regional disparities in access to trauma care across Cook County. The southern region remains entirely without trauma care infrastructure, forcing patients to travel more than 20 minutes for care following a trauma incident. This memo diagnoses the issue using a five-step sequential model and evaluates four policy alternatives through a weighted framework: Effectiveness (40%), Feasibility (20%), Cost (20%), and Time to Implement (20%). The recommended option is a Decentralized Trauma Response Network — satellite units implementable within 6–12 months at $3.75–4.95M, far more cost-effective than a full trauma center at $20–50M annually.



  Problem Definition
  Since the closure of St. James Olympia Fields in 2008, the Southland has had no trauma center, forcing patients to be transported more than 20 minutes to reach appropriate care. A 2015 study by the Illinois Department of Public Health found that trauma patients injured more than five miles from a trauma center face a 23% higher risk of death. Progress on establishing a new center has stalled due to projected annual operating costs exceeding $20 million.
  
    
    Figure 1. Geographic distribution of trauma centers (red squares) and incident density (blue dots) in Cook County. The southern region shows high incident density with no nearby trauma centers.
  



  Problem Diagnosis Model
  This issue can be structurally diagnosed through a five-step sequential process. Three stages were identified as key policy intervention points.

  
    Trauma Incident Occurrence
    →
    EMS Dispatch & On-Scene Arrival
    →
    Hospital Transport Decision
    →
    Patient Transport
    →
    Hospital Arrival & Trauma Care
  

  
    
      01
      EMS Dispatch: Response times range 15–30 min in southern Cook County, well beyond the 8-min national standard.
    
    
      02
      Transport Decision: No trauma centers in the Southland forces EMS to transport 10–15+ miles to the nearest facility.
    
    
      03
      Patient Transport: Transport durations of 17–30 min. Conditions deteriorate in transit; fatalities occur en route.
    
  



  Policy Alternatives
  
    
      Alternative 01
      Establishing a Trauma Center in the Southland
      Full trauma center in the Southland. High effectiveness but costs $20–50M annually and takes 2–5 years to implement.
      14 / 20
    
    
      Alternative 02 · Recommended
      Decentralized Trauma Response Network
      Satellite units providing stabilization and triage before transfer. Lower cost, implementable within 6–12 months.
      20 / 20
    
    
      Alternative 03
      EMS Resource Reallocation & Dispatch Protocol
      Reallocate EMS to high-risk areas. Low cost and fast to implement but doesn't address infrastructure gaps.
      18 / 20
    
    
      Alternative 04
      Air Medical Transport System
      Helicopter-based transport to bypass distance barriers. Effective but high operating costs and weather limitations.
      16 / 20
    
  



  BOTEC Cost Estimate
  First-year setup and operating cost for a single satellite trauma unit:
  



Category
Items
Estimated Cost




Facility Renovation
ER expansion, infrastructure, HVAC
$650,000 – $950,000


Medical Equipment
Trauma beds, monitors, diagnostics
$1,050,000 – $1,350,000


Personnel (annual)
Physicians, nurses, paramedics, admin
$1,300,000 – $1,700,000


Operational Costs
Supplies, utilities, maintenance, IT
$750,000 – $950,000


Total
$3,750,000 – $4,950,000







  Decision Matrix
  Alternatives were scored on four weighted criteria. Effectiveness was weighted most heavily (40%) given the primary goal of reducing mortality disparities.
  



Alternative
Effectiveness (40%)
Feasibility (20%)
Cost (20%)
Time (20%)
Score




Satellite Units
4
4
4
4
20


EMS Reallocation
2
4
5
5
18


Air Transport
4
3
2
3
16


Trauma Center
5
2
1
1
14







  Policy Evaluation

  
    
      1. Establishing a Trauma Center in the Southland
      14 / 20
    
    
      Effectiveness · 5pts — Significantly reduces transport time and mortality. Relieves pressure on downtown centers.
      Feasibility · 2pts — Complex approvals, land acquisition, staff recruitment. Lengthy multi-year process.
      Cost · 1pt — $20–50M annually. High capital and operating burden for Cook County.
      Time · 1pt — 2–5 years to full implementation.
    
  

  
    
      2. Decentralized Trauma Response Network
      20 / 20
    
    
      Effectiveness · 4pts — Triage, stabilization, pre-transfer care. Reduces transport time and improves outcomes.
      Feasibility · 4pts — MOU-based agreements with existing centers. Administratively simpler than new construction.
      Cost · 4pts — Significantly lower than a full center. Manageable operating expenses.
      Time · 4pts — Operational within 6–12 months.
    
  

  
    
      3. EMS Resource Reallocation & Dispatch Protocol
      18 / 20
    
    
      Effectiveness · 2pts — Faster on-scene care but doesn't address infrastructure gaps.
      Feasibility · 4pts — No hospital approvals needed. Administratively straightforward.
      Cost · 5pts — Minimal cost. Primarily personnel redistribution.
      Time · 5pts — Implementable within weeks.
    
  

  
    
      4. Air Medical Transport System
      16 / 20
    
    
      Effectiveness · 4pts — Bypasses traffic, significantly shortens prehospital time.
      Feasibility · 3pts — FAA compliance, helipad construction, community concerns.
      Cost · 2pts — $2–5M per helicopter annually.
      Time · 3pts — 6–12 months, subject to regulatory approval.
    
  



  Policy Recommendation
  I recommend the implementation of a Decentralized Trauma Response Network by establishing satellite trauma units in the Southland region of Cook County. These units, in collaboration with major trauma centers, would provide core services such as initial emergency stabilization, triage, and pre-transfer care. Although not full-scale trauma centers, these facilities can enable timely interventions during the critical window following trauma, significantly increasing patient survival rates.
  From a policy and administrative standpoint, this alternative is highly feasible and could be implemented within 6 to 12 months. Implementation should proceed through the following steps:
  
    
      The Cook County Board of Commissioners should formally prioritize trauma care equity and adopt a resolution supporting the establishment of satellite trauma units.
      MOUs should be signed between the County and existing trauma centers to designate appropriate partner hospitals in the Southland.
      Approval from the Illinois Department of Public Health must be obtained to verify that facility modifications comply with emergency medical service standards.
      Budget allocation should proceed in parallel. According to BOTEC, the total cost is estimated at $3,750,000–$4,950,000 — far more cost-effective than the $20–50M annual cost of a full-scale trauma center.
    
  
  This option offers a realistic and phased solution to structural disparities by reducing transport times, enabling early stabilization, and expanding emergency service capacity in underserved, high-risk areas. Furthermore, it can be integrated with future interventions such as EMS resource reallocation or air medical transport systems, increasing its policy flexibility and long-term impact.

title: When More Funding Is Not Enough: Revenue Concentration and Labor Investment in Nonprofit Performing Arts Organizations
text:
Back to projects
    

    
      
        Academic Paper
        50th Annual STP&A Conference · October 2026
      
      
        Fixed Effects · HHI Analysis
        Nonprofit Labor · Finance · Arts Policy
        R · fixest · Quarto
      
      When More Funding Is Not Enough: Revenue Concentration and Labor Investment in Nonprofit Performing Arts Organizations
      Panel data analysis examining how public funding, private donations, and revenue concentration shape personnel expenses in nonprofit performing arts organizations, using the SMU DataArts National Dataset (2019–2024).
      
  Accepted for presentation at the 50th Annual STP&A Conference (October 2026) following peer review.

      
        
        Co-authored with Lizzy Jeong and Marietta Lopez Moreira. My contributions: data cleaning, fixed-effects regression analysis and robustness checks in R, and the Data, Methods, and Results sections.
      
    

    
      
         Read Full Paper
      
    

    
      
        Year
        2026
      
      
        Type
        Course Assignment
      
      
        Course
        Advanced Data Analysis
      
      
        Tools
        R · fixest · modelsummary · Quarto
      
    

    
  
    2019–2024
    fiscal years covered
  
  
    4,131
    organization-year observations
  
  
    7
    NTEE performing arts categories analyzed
  


    
      Background
      Nonprofit performing arts organizations are labor-intensive: cultural production depends on artists, administrative staff, and production crews. Yet many operate under uncertain financial conditions, balancing public funding, private donations, and earned revenue. Whether their funding structure provides room to invest in people is therefore a pressing policy question.
      This study connects nonprofit finance, urban cultural policy, and resource dependence theory. Drawing on Pfeffer & Salancik (1978), it argues that heavy reliance on a single revenue source may reduce organizational discretion — constraining labor investment even when total resources are adequate.
    

    
      Key findings
      
        
          
            
          
          Private donations have the strongest link to personnel spending. A 1% increase in private donations is associated with a 0.130% increase in total personnel expenses (p < .001) — nearly 6× the effect of public funding — suggesting private giving converts more directly into labor investment.
        
        
          
            
          
          Public funding has a modest but significant association. A 1% increase in public funding is associated with a 0.022% increase in personnel expenses (p < .01), though this association does not hold in the W-2 robustness check, suggesting restrictions may limit direct labor use.
        
        
          
            
          
          Revenue concentration is the strongest predictor overall. Holding total revenue constant, a 0.1-unit increase in HHI is associated with a 4.4% decrease in personnel expenses (p < .001), confirmed by the W-2 robustness check.
        
      
    

   
  
    Table 1. Funding Sources and Personnel Expenses
    
  
  
    Table 2. Revenue Concentration and Personnel Expenses
    
  


    
      Implications
      For arts nonprofit managers: financial strategies should go beyond increasing revenue to actively managing revenue composition — over-reliance on a single source limits workforce flexibility even when resources are available. For funders and policymakers: supporting more balanced funding portfolios may strengthen these organizations' capacity to invest in labor and contribute to cultural and community development goals.
      
        Revenue diversification may be a key condition for a more sustainable nonprofit arts workforce — more funding matters, but funding structure matters as well.

title: Organizational Capacity as a Policy Intervention Point: Financial Sustainability in U.S. Nonprofit Theater Organizations
text:
Back to projects
    

    
      
        Featured
        AAAE 47th Annual Conference · May 2026
      
      
        Mixed Methods Research
        Regression · In-Depth Interviews
        Nonprofit Finance · Arts Policy
        R
      
      Organizational Capacity as a Policy Intervention Point: Financial Sustainability in U.S. Nonprofit Theater Organizations
      What began as a Chicago-focused independent study grew into a national analysis — starting with in-depth interviews with 5 local theater organizations, then expanding to quantitative analysis of 457 U.S. nonprofits to examine how revenue structures and organizational capacity shape financial sustainability.
    

    
      
         Independent Study Report
      
      
         Full Paper (in progress)
      
    

    
      
        Year
        2025–2026
      
      
        Type
        Independent Study
      
      
        Outcome
        Presented at AAAE 47th Annual Conference · May 2026
      
      
        Tools
        R
      
    

    
  
    2019–2023
    fiscal years covered
  
  
    457
    U.S. nonprofit theater organizations analyzed
  
  
    5
    Chicago theater organizations interviewed
  


    
      Project timeline
      
        
          
          Oct – Nov 2025
          Exploratory interviews
          Conducted in-depth interviews with 5 Chicago nonprofit theater organizations to identify structural constraints on earned-revenue generation and financial sustainability.
        
        
          
          December 2025
          Independent Study Report
          Cross-sectional analysis of 69 Chicago theaters — examining revenue structure types, deficit ratios, and operational characteristics. Supervised by Professor Yasen Peyankov, University of Illinois Chicago.
        
        
          
          Spring 2026
          Expanded to national scale
          Extended the quantitative analysis to 457 U.S. nonprofit theater organizations and conducted an expanded literature review.
        
        
          
          May 2026
          Presented at AAAE 47th Annual Conference
          Presented findings in Pecha Kucha format at the Association for Arts Administration Educators 47th Annual Conference.
          
        
        
          
          June 2026
          Complete paper coming soon
        
      
    

 
  Key findings
  
    
      
        
      
      Workforce size is positively associated with earned revenue. A one-unit increase in log workforce size is associated with a 5.2 percentage point increase in the earned revenue ratio (β=0.052, p<.001), supporting the human capital hypothesis.
    
    
      
        
      
      Performance volume also matters. A one-unit increase in log performance occurrences is associated with a 3.4 percentage point increase in the earned revenue ratio (β=0.034, p<.001), consistent with the programmatic capital hypothesis.
    
    
      
        
      
      Space ownership shows no significant association with earned revenue. Contrary to expectations, whether an organization owns or rents its space is not meaningfully associated with the earned revenue ratio — suggesting physical capital operates through indirect mechanisms.
    
    
      
        
      
      Organizations with greater financial reserves earn less from the market — consistent with the starvation cycle. A higher equity ratio is negatively associated with earned revenue (β=−0.021, p<.001), suggesting that organizations relying on contributed income may underinvest in capacity, reinforcing dependence on external funding.
    
  


    
  Implications
  The findings suggest that public arts policy should function as capacity investment, not revenue substitution. When grants cover only program costs without supporting staffing or operational infrastructure, they risk deepening — rather than breaking — the nonprofit starvation cycle.
  
    
      
        
      
      Prioritize workforce investment. Shift from project-based grants to general operating support that explicitly covers personnel costs — the dimension most directly linked to earned revenue capacity.
    
    
      
        
      
      Support programmatic infrastructure. Touring grants, co-production funding, and multi-year program support can expand performance volume — the second strongest predictor of earned revenue.
    
    
      
        
      
      Reconsider facility-focused policy. Space ownership shows no direct association with earned revenue. Policies centered on physical infrastructure may be less effective than those targeting operational capacity.
    
    
      
        
      
      Design grants to break the starvation cycle. Funding structures should enable overhead investment — treating administrative and operational capacity as preconditions for self-reliance, not as waste.
    
  
  Policy should function as capacity investment, not revenue substitution — shifting from short-term program outputs toward the organizational conditions that make earned revenue growth possible.

title: Student Enrollment Patterns in a Public Affairs Graduate Program: A 10-Year Trend Analysis
text:
Back to projects
    

    
      
        Capstone Project
        Descriptive Statistics · Trend Analysis
        Higher Ed · Program Evaluation
        R · Tableau · Quarto
      
      Student Enrollment Patterns in a Public Affairs Graduate Program: A 10-Year Trend Analysis
      Analysis of 10 years of student enrollment records across UIC's MPA, MPP, and MSCA programs — examining course sequencing, course load, time to degree, and shifting subject preferences. Presented to the PPMA department.
      
        
        Co-authored with Ronnie Chen and Imaan Jahangir. My contributions: data cleaning, reproducible Quarto report, visualizations, and Q1 (course sequencing), Q3 (time to degree), and Q7 (course retakes) analyses.
      
    

    
      
         Read Full Report
      
      
         View Code
      
    

    
      
        Year
        2026
      
      
        Type
        Capstone Project
      
      
        Course
        PA590
      
      
        Tools
        R · Tableau · Quarto
      
    

    
      
        2015–2025
        academic years covered
      
      
        3
        graduate programs analyzed (MPA, MPP, MSCA)
      
      
        7
        research questions examined
      
    

    
      Research questions
      
        Q1Are students following the recommended course sequence?
        Q2Are there signs of course overload?
        Q3How has time to degree changed over time?
        Q4How have enrollment patterns across concentrations shifted?
        Q5How much do students deviate from the recommended course plan?
        Q6How has participation in nonprofit coursework changed?
        Q7To what extent do students retake courses?
      
    

    
  Visualizations (selected examples)
      
        
      
      
        
      
    

    
      Key findings
      
        
          
            
          
          Students largely follow the intended curriculum structure. Most complete foundational courses within their first two available opportunities, with timing variation driven by course scheduling rather than intentional deviation.
        
        
          
            
          
          Time to degree is stable and aligned with program expectations. Most students complete within 2.5–3 years. Recent cohorts show both faster and more consistent completion, with 86% of 2024–2025 graduates finishing within 3 years.
        
        
          
            
          
          Demand for data and analytics coursework is growing. Enrollments in methods and analytics courses have increased steadily, while nonprofit and governance courses remain stable but lower in participation.
        
        
          
            
          
          Course retakes are uncommon and concentrated. Only 15.7% of students retook any course, with retakes concentrated in a small number of foundational courses (PA 504, PA 506, PA 401).
        
      
    

    
      Post-presentation analysis
      Following the final presentation, additional analysis was conducted in response to feedback from the client meeting. The findings reveal a connection between course sequencing, overload, and retake likelihood.
      
        
          
            
          
          Students who retake courses carry significantly higher course loads. Retake students average 15.22 credits per term vs. 10.79 for non-retake students, and take more courses per term (3.86 vs. 2.74).
        
        
          
            
          
          66.7% of retaken courses were first taken under overload conditions (more than 12 credits), with a mean credit load of 15.75 at first attempt.
        
        
          
            
          
          Taking PA 504 before PA 403 increases the likelihood of retaking PA 504. Students who took PA 504 before PA 403 retook it at 2.5%, compared to 0.6% for those who followed the recommended sequence — 4× higher.
        
      
      
  
  
      
    

    
      Implications
      The findings suggest that student progression is shaped more by program design and course availability than by individual student behavior. Expanding high-demand sections in analytics and finance, and more intentional course scheduling, could reduce overload and better align availability with student demand.
      Structural factors — course availability and program design — are the primary drivers of how students navigate the curriculum.

title: Arts Education Equity in Chicago
text:
← Back to projects
  
  
    Data Analysis Report
    Regression · Spatial Analysis
    Arts Education · Equity
    R · Shiny · Quarto · GitHub
  
  Arts Education Equity in Chicago: A Data Analysis of Arts Access in Public Schools
  ZIP code-level analysis of Creative Schools Certification (CSC) scores across Chicago, combining ACS data, spatial mapping, regression, an interactive Shiny dashboard, and a decision tree classifier to examine how socioeconomic conditions shape arts education access.



  
     Read Full Report
  
  
     View Code
  



  Year2025
  TypeCourse Assignment
  CourseCoding Civic Data Applications
  ToolsR · tidycensus · Shiny · Quarto



  Overview
  Although CPS designates the arts as a "core subject," the quality and availability of arts instruction vary considerably across neighborhoods. Arts teacher vacancies are disproportionately concentrated in South and West Side neighborhoods, many schools do not meet the required 120 minutes of weekly arts instruction, and facility disparities remain persistent across the district.
  
    "How are neighborhood-level socioeconomic factors associated with Creative Schools Certification (CSC) outcomes across Chicago ZIP codes, and what do these relationships imply for advancing arts education equity in CPS?"
  



  Analytical Approach
  This project merges ZIP code-level socioeconomic indicators from the American Community Survey (ACS) 2022 5-year estimates with Creative Schools Certification (CSC) data from Ingenuity's ArtLook Maps. The analysis proceeds in four steps:
  
    
      01
      Spatial mapping of CSC scores by ZIP code
    
    
      02
      OLS regression — 4 socioeconomic predictors
    
    
      03
      Interactive Shiny dashboard for stakeholder exploration
    
    
      04
      Decision tree classifier to identify combinational patterns
    
  



  Visualizations
  
    
      
      Figure 1. Average CSC Score by ZIP Code. Higher scores concentrated in the North Side; lower scores in South and West Sides.
    
    
      
      Figure 2. Regression coefficient plot. Most predictors show weak associations with wide confidence intervals crossing zero.
    
    
      
      Figure 3. Interactive Shiny dashboard allowing stakeholders to explore relationships between socioeconomic predictors and CSC scores.
    
    
      
      Figure 4. Decision tree classifier. Educational attainment (pct_noncollege) emerged as the only meaningful split — ZIP codes with pct_noncollege < 80% classified as High CSC with 83% probability.
    
  



  Key Findings
  
    
      01
      Clear North-South divide — higher CSC scores in North and central ZIP codes, lower scores in South and West Sides.
    
    
      02
      Poverty rate, racial composition, and linguistic diversity show weak, statistically insignificant associations with CSC scores (R² = 0.55).
    
    
      03
      Educational attainment was the only meaningful predictor in the decision tree — ZIP codes with pct_noncollege < 80% classified as High CSC with 83% probability.
    
    
      04
      Disparities are likely driven by structural factors — staffing, budgets, partnership networks — not ZIP-level demographics alone.
    
  



  Policy Implications
  
    
      Prioritize structural needs
      Direct arts educators and resources to ZIP codes with staffing shortages and facility limitations, not based on demographic characteristics.
    
    
      Expand community partnerships
      Facilitate more arts partnerships for historically under-resourced schools to mitigate staffing and facility gaps.
    
    
      Improve data transparency
      Support schools with CSC reporting and integrate real-time indicators such as staffing stability and partner engagement.
    
    
      Center equity in policy design
      Lower CSC scores reflect structural conditions, not community deficiencies. Results should guide support, not punitive accountability measures.
    
  



  Limitations
  
    This analysis relies on ZIP code-level aggregates, which may mask within-ZIP variation. CSC data depends on school self-reporting, and schools with incomplete submissions — often those with fewer staff — may be underrepresented. ACS estimates are five-year averages that may not reflect recent neighborhood changes. Findings should be read as diagnostic signals, not causal claims.
